comments.title: Sources on Shintoism and Hinduism
comments.content:
Good evening!

Thanks as always for providing this venue and sharing your knowledge. In the interest of giving as well as receiving, I've tried to share something hopefully useful to you and/or the commentariat along with each of my questions tonight, as both are for you and/or the commentariat:

1) Do you (or anyone in the commentariat) have any recommendations for sources on Shinto? Ideally, something with details on actual practice and ritual, though I know that is hugely localized. My own offering: the Tsubaki Grand Shrine of America that you've mentioned before hosts a book by one of their former prominent priests, the "Kami No Michi" (Way of the Kami), which I haven't dug much into yet, but looks quite interesting and includes a set of spiritual exercises in the appendix: http://www.tsubakishrine.org/kaminomichi/index.html

2) Similar to the above, but separated for easier reference, do you (or anyone in the commentariat) have any recommendations on sources for Hinduism? The linking thread between these two is that they're both major, relatively unbroken polytheistic/nature-based religions, and I'd like to get a better idea bout what that actually looks like without "reconstructionist" lenses. As my own source, I'll share Mircea Eliade's Yoga: Immortality and Freedom, which struck me as a pretty comprehensive history of Yoga, but coming from the standpoint of somoene who at least respects it, if not actively follows it, paired with Eliade's massive cross-cultural knowledge of religious practices and beliefs.

I hope my shared sources might help some folks, and thank you JMG and anyone else with some further sources to share.

Cheers,
Jeff

You all might be interested in Denny Sargent's book "Dancing With Spirit's: the festivals and folklore of Japan". Denny Sargent, aka Aion 131 is a founding member of Horus-Maat Lodge with a long background in Western magic, and he his wife lived in Japan for awhile if I recall correctly. This book, I believe, came out of that time spent there.

"Nowhere are religious traditions more uniquely tied to the historical, cultural and spiritual identity of a people then in Japan. Almost all Japanese follow one intertwined religious mixture of Shinto, Matsuri and Buddhism. This forms the basis of much of their culture. To know this is to know Japan. Dancing with Spirits is an intellectual but accessible 'travel guide' through the history and fun reality of the most important Shinto, Matsuri and Buddhist festivals of Japan. It features entertaining first-hand accounts of wild revels like Tanabata and Setsubun, but it also offers something more; the history of the these festivals and explanations of the symbolism and meaning behind them, things that even many of the Japanese revelers themselves often don't know. Other aspects of Buddhism, Matsuri and Shinto are covered within these pages as well, from a succinct history of the origins of these great traditions in Japan to chapters about specific deities and Amulets. Come and leap into the amazing world of Shinto, Matsuri and Buddhism and the 'floating world' of Japanese rituals and folklore as it is being celebrated today! Dozo!"

(Among his other books, "The Book of The Horned One: A Gate of Pan Magick" as Aion 131 is great for anyone who feels called to Pan...)

Justin Patrick Moore

Three books on aspects of religion in India (Hindu-related) that I highly recommend:

Offering Flowers, Feeding Skulls by June McDaniel

-This one is about folk/tribal Hindu practices around Shakti/goddess worship in Bengal. Very interesting sections on stones which are known to be the home of goddesses and how these goddesses make their locations known (often via dream visitation).

https://www.amazon.com/Offering-Flowers-Feeding-Skulls-Popular/dp/0195167910

Nine Lives by William Dalrymple

Incredible work of oral history interviewing several different spiritual practitioners in India (not all are Hindu). Detailed.

https://www.amazon.com/Nine-Lives-Search-Vintage-Departures/dp/0307474461/ref=mp_s_a_1_1?crid=1VNTPJ3QA8BMD&keywords=nine+lives+dalrymple&qid=1649678561&sprefix=nine+lives+dal%2Caps%2C151&sr=8-1

Living Without the Dead by Piers Vitebsky

Deeply empathetic and sensitive book about the Sora people, a tribal Indian people who until very recently practiced an intense medium-based religion but are now converting to orthodox Hinduism and Baptist Christianity. (This book is sad.)

https://www.amazon.com/Living-without-Dead-Redemption-Jungle-ebook/dp/B075VT2JQQ/ref=mp_s_a_1_2?crid=2BBIWU0E29RI3&keywords=piers+vitebsky&qid=1649678672&sprefix=viteb%2Caps%2C98&sr=8-2

There are many resources on traditional or philosophical Hinduism but I find these three books, among others, fascinating for their discussion of vernacular, lived forms of Hinduism (and its fringes). Lots of discussion in these on occult and spiritual experiences and ritual.

2) I too have been directed toward India in the search for knowledge of polytheist traditions, and am now studying Sanskrit. One point of contact between Indian traditions and Western esoteric traditions is the classical planets (the Navagraha - the planets plus lunar nodes); there are temples and shrines to the planets, and an unbroken tradition of planetary devotion; they rule the same days (Saturn - Saturday, Jupiter - Thursday, etc.) as and share many attributes with the classical planets in the West.
I recommend Robert Svoboda's The Greatness of Saturn: A Therapeutic Myth as an introduction to this aspect of Hinduism.
If you're interested, try researching Navagraha temples and worship:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Navagraha_temples

replies.title: Re: Sources on Shintoism and Hinduism
replies.content:
My favorite books about Shinto that I've read are Carmen Blacker's "The Catalpa Bow" and Karen Smyers's "The Fox and the Jewel". They're both erudite, readable, and manage to convey a sense of the spiritual power that resides in Shinto. I also recommend the blog Green Shinto, now mostly inactive but with a wealth of information, and also the website "Gods of Japan: A-Z Photo Dictionary of Japanese Religious Sculpture and Art".

As far as Shinto source texts go, the two primary texts are the Kojiki and the Nihongi (aka the Nihon Shoki), which were recorded at very nearly the same time-- the general consensus is that the Kojiki came first, though there are some who disagree. Right now, due to historical reasons that were in part responsible for the split between Buddhism and Shinto in the 19th century, the Kojiki has come to predominate as far as fame with the the general public goes. Through most of Japan's history, though, it seems that the Nihongi was more considered the first place to go for the mythology, often offering multiple versions of the same mythological stories for comparison within the text itself (but missing some crucial stories that appear in the Kojiki, including one of my favorites, The Hare of Inaba).

If one is just dipping their toes into Shinto, I strongly recommend the Heldt translation of the Kojiki, which keeps its language simple but pleasant, and is of a length that isn't too daunting. One quibble though, it has a major weakness in that all the kami and place names are translated as their literal meanings in English, rather than by the names that anybody who actually prays to these great spirits actually uses to address them with (though there is a glossary in the back). If you need to go for a free version of the Kojiki, the original Basil Hall Chamberlain version is readily available, though it suffers for many of the same problems as JMG has lamented with Waite's translation of Levi.

The only currently extant English translation of the Nihongi, by W.G. Aston, is also a Victorian translation, but I find it quite a bit more readable than Chamberlain's Kojiki.

A few questions for tonight:

1) Is the feeling of “detachment” one gains from consistent magical practice effectively a low-grade form of mystical experience?

2) Does the feeling of detachment keep increasing indefinitely with practice? If so, at what point do we become so spaced out as to be non-functional for daily living?

3) To what extent are more complex, practical workings necessary to develop as a magician? If someone does only the basic preparatory practices (e.g. LBRP, MP, divination, meditation, or their equivalents in another tradition), could they reach the same degree of spiritual development as a thaumaturgist who does the preparatory practices in addition to more practical workings, e.g. for success, health, love? Can one go all the way to adepthood without any practical magic whatsoever?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) It depends very much on what you mean by that remarkably flexible phrase "mystical experience."

2) Not in my experience. Once you realize that your body, your personality, and your material surroundings are temporary and rather flimsy shells surrounding your real self, you get used to that, and then you can use them constructively rather than being used by them.

3) They're not necessary at all. You can achieve the heights of magical attainment without ever casting a spell for any practical purpose, and you'll do it faster than the person who lingers in the byways of practical magic; it's just that most people don't have that singleness of purpose. (I certainly didn't when I got started.)

replies.title: Re: Object inspired
replies.content:
1) I haven't heard any recent news; I'll drop an email to the publisher and see what's up.

2) Of all the prophetic religions of the Piscean era, Islam went the furthest in rejecting the old temple cults; sacramental Christianity and Buddhism both preserved some elements of the old temple tradition, by contrast. All the prophetic religions are dogmatic, in that they consider belief in a set of opinions about the universe to be the basis of salvation: this is as true of the Buddhist dharma as of the Christian gospel and the Muslim aqidah, though of course in all three faiths there's a lot of variation on how strictly the dogmas are defined and enforced.

3) Muslim societies have very rich traditions of occultism and magic. JMG; I hope you are well. And thank you for all the wisdom you impart on the many forums you host.

A few observations for your consideration from a practicing muslim, one with some interest in the mystical/occult aspects of it.
a) While dogmatic Islam certainly has tendencies towards intolerance of other religions/practices, esoteric Islam (as for example exemplified by Sufism) is very open-ended (with a live-and-let-live philosophy). It’s almost like Hinduism (see Frijhof Schoen’s, ‘the transcendent unity of religions’ for further elucidation, for example). The detailed doctrinal basis of this tendency towards tolerance in Islam goes back all the way to Ibn Arabi’s thought; he is also known as ‘sheikh e Akbar’, or the ‘greatest master’ in Sufism. For him, all of creation with all its variation and religions and colors, is a Manifestation of the One Reality (the core doctrine of ‘wahdat-Al-wajud’, or ‘the Oneness of Being’). And further back to even the Quran itself, where select verses overtly validate other ‘revealed’ religions. And thus it is not surprising that the shrines of muslim saints in the Indian subcontinent (Sehwan Sharif for example) are sites of pilgrimage for Hindus and Christians as well. And this similarly explains why many a muslim may travel to and venerate a Christian holy/shrine. The good news is, that in most Islamic lands, it is typically the folk/Sufi religious practices that predominate within a population et large rather than the dogmatic ones (in general, more people travel to shrines than to mosques). In short, Islam as you already know, is much more complex than what is presented on MSM. And historically, often a time, ‘the other’ has found its lands to be relatively quite welcoming.
b) ‘Shirk’ in Islam is a loaded term. While overtly it certainly means to stand against polytheism; it’s much farther-reaching practical import for a muslim is to deny the worship of one’s own Ego (or ‘nafs-e-ammara’; again, this goes back to the Quran). As we all know, one’s Ego more often than not acts as the false god/idol (‘My’ fame, ‘My’ power, ‘My’ wealth, and so on) the worship of which ends up being the cause of much strife and disharmony in the cosmos; and it’s nullification is considered a prerequisite towards attaining enlightenment in mystical Islam. In day to day life of a muslim, this is the ‘shirk’ that by far outcompetes other considerations.
c) the Sufi masters said that God gives sunshine to the rich and the poor, and rain to the good and the bad (or, the unvaccinated and the vaccinated alike, if one prefers). And if man/woman is to realize one’s theomorphic nature (Ie become in His image), one ought to act so as well. Obviously, a polytheist would risk persecution in a land inhabited by Muslims; there is no denying it. But I hope and pray, but am also certain, that he/she will also find many a friend and protector therein as well.

Prayer and best wishes as always.
m

Welcome to the basic challenge faced by every human being, and especially by every human being who decides to become an individual instead of a puppet of the mass mind. We all have to confront this challenge; the only people who are born with a relatively unified will went through the same mess you're facing in a previous life.

One day, we went to a place called Canyon de Chelly. We were standing on a cliff, looking out over this massive canyon filled with the most lovely windcaves, and I thought, as though it was the most normal and obvious thing in the world, I am a dragon. I could physically feel my wings, scales, claws and tail, and knew that I needed to fly, to take one of these caves for my own and raise my hatchlings...
I spread my arms wide, feeling my wings billow in the air...and was pulled back from the edge of the cliff.
Luckily there was a safety railing on it, too! I climbed on top of a boulder to try and get above it, but was told to get off the boulder and come stay with the group, which was probably for the best.

Any idea, between you and the commentariat, what happened? I've always been imaginative, but it never had gone that far before, and has never gone quite like that since.

replies.title: Re: Crystal glass
replies.content:
It's a funny little detail of material science that the term "crystal glass" is actually an oxymoron. Glass is a liquid -- a very, very thick liquid, which is why it takes centuries to flow -- but it's a liquid and therefore has no crystalline structure in it.

Glass generally has no significant magical properties, so you should be fine.

Others will leave you stone cold dead -- some of the attempts at kundalini yoga by Western practitioners are examples here. It's a crapshoot; doubtless people will keep experimenting, and the survivors will pass on what they've learned.

replies.title: Do not think too much about that
replies.content:
Why 12? why 7? It is hard to say but I am pretty sure it has to do with "why not 469471?".

Small numbers are meaningful because our minds perceive them more significant, but why? Maybe it is that there's an underlying potency that manifest itself in pure abstract form in precisely those numbers. If it is so, different cultures will keep rediscovering those over and over again. It is also possible that there's an element of cultural cross-fertilization so to speak, that the ancient nations had ties between them that our current biases prevent us from noticing.

In any case, learn each system one at a time, and see what correspondences jump in front of your eyes. But no, just "twelve" means little if anything at all.

comments.title: Inarticulate Incarnation
comments.content:
Thank you for being here, and for doing this.

You replied to a question of mine a couple weeks ago: "...the forms taken by that noetic entity on the plane we can reach are different, and so you're better off treating them as different beings. ... each noetic entity is an reflection on the noetic plane of some aspect of the One, the impersonal transcendent unity on the henadic plane, but then so is every other noetic entity including your higher self and mine..."

It seems like this is pointing in some way toward a similar understanding of incarnated humans. That while I can know that each person has a higher self that transcends the pettiness of incarnated energy struggles, it doesn't necessarily behove me to actually deal with each person as though they ARE their higher self, because that could lead to being swindled or hurt by their current incarnation who is learning about power dynamics.

I had a discussion years ago with someone arguing that "WE ARE ALL ONE" and we just needed to start acting like it. I thought on it and responded that though "we are all one" might be true at some level, it won't likely help to pretend that this level (on which we are many) is that level as that would be a delusion. This led to further discussion on my opinion that it seems preferable to do beneficial works with my body while I have one, rather than trying to surpass or bypass it; though my friend made other points on that I continue to ponder.

Questions:
1) You've been discussing (in other answers) magical dissociation, and that the sensing both your higher self and your current body is a sense that you can use to have more agency for both. In your understanding, is the concept of oneness at a universal level similarly a sense/tool/path, or is it a different concept, or even an end-point (i.e. we incarnate seeking a path back to the universal oneness)?

2) I understand that we can use meditation and magical work to better understand our own karma. My meditation suggests that this benefits us not because we can circumvent anything, but because we can then see karmic lessons in our path and be in a willing state to work through them and allow them to teach us. This also seems it would benefit us with gifts of empathy for others learning from Karma.

If we all have lessons to learn, do we all suffer from similar mistakes, or are there higher-selves that make it through their incarnations on a relatively balanced and beneficial side of the karmic without doing horrific things in their incarnations? I guess this also leads into a question of morality in Karmic terms.

3) On what level does morality exist? Is there morality to karma, or just recompense? It doesn't seem morality exists per se on the physical level where killing/being-killed happens by the millions every day. Though I suppose eating to survive and later being eaten by the worms is a nice karmic package. Perhaps karma exists on every level...? Can it cross levels? Could you pay for physical karma on the etheric level? Oi. I just thought of embalming/caskets and all the beings deprived of human-body meals, what kind of karma is is to eat through life and then deprive the world of your meat? Uh-oh. I think I've begun discursively meditating through my question. Perhaps you could just touch on the first question of this string...

4) I'm concerned with the ethics of self-protection, which seems it can often walk in a karmic liminal space. I practice aikido (though it's a less traditional and more martial form;) and I'm pondering how to, in spiritual terms, protect myself and those working against me, so that I don't accumulate more painful lessons through my own defense. I suppose it's along the lines of your recommendation - work positive magic on oneself, rather than negative magic on others, but extended to all areas of my life. Very similar to aikido in that learning to maintain one's own balance, posture, and groundedness is essential. I guess this is part of the work of priorities and will - deciding which karma I'm willing to endure to protect this incarnation.

replies.title: Re: Inarticulate Incarnation
replies.content:
1) It's a much more advanced concept, and like most such, it can be a source of embarrassing mistakes if taken too seriously too early. All things may be one, but that doesn't mean you can eat granite!

2) It's theoretically possible that a soul could pass all the way through its human incarnations without making any serious mistakes. It might even happen, too -- once, or maybe twice, in the history of our species. The rest of us just have to muddle on making and learning from our mistakes.

3) Morality is a set of rules established by a culture to govern human relationships within that culture. Karma is the simple fact that your actions shape your future. They don't actually have that much to do with one another, though the Piscean era's fixation on dogmatic morality makes that hard to see. In the world of manifestation, everything that lives feeds on other life forms -- even plants send their root hairs by preference to feed on the bodies of animals and plants in the soil. All things have died that you might feed on them, and you're being eaten right now -- there are bacteria dining on you as we speak. None of that is relevant to morality. It doesn't have a lot to do with karma, either!

4) There's no virtue in letting yourself be abused. Self-defense is karmically positive -- if you defend yourself from attack, you help create a world where people can defend themselves from attack -- and defending the weak and vulnerable is even more karmically positive. Abusing your martial skills to hurt other people who haven't attacked you is a bad idea, of course, but that's not what aikido teaches!

replies.title: Re: Inarticulate Incarnation
replies.content:
1) LOL, that jives with my feelings on it.
2) The theoretical possibility helps inform my understanding of the concept, thank you.
3) Lots of food for meditation here. Thank you for indulging my train of thought and untangling those concepts.
4) It's a relief to read that. I felt that self-defense, even if it sadly resulted in another's harm, would be karmically positive, but my thoughts kept working at it - perhaps because of the unhelpful linking of morality and karma addressed above. On a side note - I find the physical teachings of aikido to be very spiritually informative.

Thank you again.

comments.title: Two questions about protection
comments.content:
Hello JMG.

So, my girlfriend is naturally quite psychic and empathic. I have two questions related to this:

1. Is there any way she can stop picking up negative feelings of people close to other, other than taking up ritual magic with its protective rituals?
2. A family member of her died half a year ago. After this, she picked up a health problem that this deceases person had. She has been on very extensive tests, doctors say nothing is wrong with her physically, but the problem just persists. Could she have picked up it from this person and if so, how does she get rid of it?

replies.title: Re: Two questions about protection
replies.content:
1) The way I know of is to take up protective rituals. There may be other ways, but keep in mind I'm just an old-fashioned occultist, and I'm not omniscient!

2) Yes, she could have picked that up from the other person. Again, the way I know of getting rid of something like that involves ceremonial magic. There may be other options not known to me.

replies.title: Re: Two questions about protection
replies.content:
From my first and second hand experience:
To a person who hasn't actively worked to develop psychic or empathic qualities but has them, there seems to be a tendency to identity with those qualities. Thinking "I am psychic or I am empathic" rather than thinking "I can use these skills." I think this is tied to much of our fiction which shows people who are born wizards, witches, or magical and other people who are mundane.
With research and training, you learn both that other people can develop these skills, and that they ARE skills which means that they are not you and you can exert some will in the situation. How much will seems to differ - I know very sensitive people who have learned their limits and boundaries, but knowing that is a power in and of itself.
I share this thinking that perhaps a first step could be affirmations that acknowledge agency and control over those skills, and then beginning a path to learn one of the many arts that can develop more awareness and will with which to adapt to having those skills. Since you are here, I can highly recommend the ceremonial clearing and protection work that JMG teaches in his books and on this blog.

comments.title: Astrology for organizations
comments.content: What kind of astrology would be good for learning the fortunes of an organization or company? Does it still fall under mundane astrology? The fiction that corporations are people, or extremely small size of an organization, wouldn't bring natal astrology into it, right?

replies.title: Re: Astrology for organizations
replies.content: I'm not sure there's a specific term yet for the astrology of organizations, but it's a thing. It's closer to natal astrology than to mundane astrology -- you need to work out the horoscope of the organization, using the legal founding date; in financial astrology, noon is always taken as the founding time of a company, so you can use that in this case as well. Then you delineate the chart and track transits and progressions across it, as you would a natal chart.

comments.title: Sorting my selves out
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I'm trying to sort my selves out. Could you let me know if the following accords with your understanding?

--The Ego is that part of ourselves which consciously perceives itself and thinks about itself in the normal everyday way we are mostly accustomed to.

--According to the Jungians, the archetype of the Self includes the Ego, as well as all unconscious parts that make up the whole person, including other archetypes as they express themselves in one's life, memories, and connections to the collective unconscious.

--All of this is folded up within the Individuality, in which the current Self is the tip of an enormous mountain of previous lives that the Individuality has experienced, all of which add ingredients and facets and patterns to the current expression of life in the material world that one inhabits.

--And the Individuality is, in turn, the current expression in the realm of time of the Higher Self, which is even grander because it exists out of time, and contains within it the full set of all lives, experiences, and deeds that one's Individuality has ever, can ever, and will ever be.

Does this train of thought have any problems that you see? Particularly, are the Jungian conception of the archetype of the Self and the occult term "the Individuality" actually different enough to be called separate things?

replies.title: Re: Sorting my selves out
replies.content: Okay, you're mushing together two different systems, and I'm not sure they really fit together that way. The ego and the Self are Jung's terms. The personality and the individuality are pretty much Dion Fortune's terms for the same two things, and the individuality is also the higher self. So you may be turning two things into five...

replies.title: Re: Sorting my selves out
replies.content: Okay, thanks for the clarification. Turning two things into five is a special gift of mine, occasionally useful but probably not in this case!

comments.title: The Fiction of Manly P. Hall
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I was wondering if you were acquainted with the fiction of Manly P. Hall?

I'd heard of "When Were You Born?", the 1938 astrological mystery movie that he penned and also appeared in the introduction of. I was pleased to discover while writing this question out that the whole movie is actually currently viewable on YouTube here, so I guess I'll be giving it a watch when I have some time.

There is also a curious little book of short stories that came into my possession called "Very Unusual: The Wonderful World of Mr. K. Nakamura", set in the 1920s, about a foreigner in Kyoto (named "Hall-san") who befriends an antiques shop owner named Nakamura-san. The general formula for each story is the pair coming across some curio that has magical/occult properties, e.g. landscape paintings in which the moon slowly changes phase with accordance with the heavens, or a cursed mirror that contains the soul of a girl who committed suicide over it. The stories are generally genial and mild, though sometimes dipping their toes into other genres such as spy story or underworld crime. There are also stories which only contain the barest hint of something occult, e.g. the one where Nakamura-san performs as Santa Claus for a local kindergarten, and then at the very end: "In Buddhism, we believe that such personifications are more real than actual persons. When I play Santa Claus, I am no longer Mr. Nakamura. I am Santa Claus. You might say that I permit myself to become the embodiment of a kindly spirit that likes to bring happiness to boys and girls. The children do not ask if I am a Buddhist or a Christian. They know that I am Mr. Nakamura, but they do not care. When their young hearts are full of laughter, and they clap their hands with simple joy, I am not a person; I am an experience."

Do you happen to know of any other instances of fiction by the Manly one?

replies.title: Re: The Fiction of Manly P. Hall
replies.content: Well, there's The Ways of the Lonely Ones, and I think there are others. I haven't read all his very large output, however!

replies.title: Re: The Fiction of Manly P. Hall
replies.content: Excellent, I was hoping there was more! Thank you!
